1:1.text: The Basmala is preferred at the start of every permissible matter. To omit it from a recitation of Al-Fatiha is, for the Shafi'is, to leave the prayer incomplete, since the Basmala is counted among its verses in the school's reading.

The Basmala teaches that no act of a believer begins from himself — every action depends on Allah's enabling and is referred back to His mercy. To say Bismillah with attention is to acknowledge that dependence concretely.

1:5.text: Ibn Taymiyyah identifies this verse as the axis of the surah: the first half (1:1-4) is praise; the second half (1:6-7) is petition. Verse 1:5 is the hinge — the declaration of pure servitude that authorises the petition that follows.

2:255.text: Al-Ghazali, in Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din, treats Ayat al-Kursi as a meditation on Allah's attributes: each clause is an opening into knowing Him. To recite it without attention to its content is to handle a treasure as if it were ordinary speech.

Ibn al-Uthaymin underscores the sound hadith that whoever recites Ayat al-Kursi after every obligatory prayer, nothing prevents him from Paradise except death. He cautions that the reward is for one who recites with belief and presence, not mere repetition.
